data.title: Federman & Sherwood Investigates Sunflower Medical Group, P.A. for Data Breach
data.description:
OKLAHOMA CITY, Okla., March 12, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — The law firm of Federman & Sherwood has initiated an investigation into Sunflower Medical Group, P.A. with respect to their recent data breach. On January 7, 2025, Sunflower learned that an unauthorized third party had potentially accessed Sunflower’s systems and acquired copies of certain files. Upon learning of the incident, Sunflower promptly took steps to further secure its systems and engaged a forensic security firm to assist with Sunflower’s investigation of the incident. The investigation determined that an unknown third party accessed Sunflower’s systems on or about December 15, 2024, and acquired copies of certain files from our systems as a part of the incident. The following personal information was accessed: individuals name, address, date of birth, Social Security number, driver's license number, medical information and health insurance information.

If you wish to discuss this action, obtain further information, and/or participate in this litigation, please contact Tiffany Peintner either by email at trp@federmanlaw.com or visit our firm’s website: www.federmanlaw.com.

Tiffany Peintner

FEDERMAN & SHERWOOD

10205 North Pennsylvania Avenue

Oklahoma City, OK 73120

data.title: Canadian Pacific Kansas City Issues $1.2B Debt Offering
data.description:
By Denny Jacob

Canadian Pacific Kansas City is offering up $1.2 billion in debt.

The freight rail operator said Wednesday its subsidiary, Canadian Pacific Railway, is issuing $600 million each on notes due in 2030 and 2035, which will be guaranteed by the parent company.

Net proceeds from the offering will be used primarily for the refinancing of outstanding debt and general corporate purposes, CPKC said. The offering is expected to close on Monday.

Write to Denny Jacob at denny.jacob@wsj.com

data.title: Bluesky Digital Assets Corp. Correction Notice
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - March 12, 2025) - Bluesky Digital Assets Corp., , (OTCQB: BTCWF), ("Bluesky" or the "Corporation") announced today that it would like to correct wording previously stated in its March 7, 2025 press release. The Corporation previously stated that it had granted 6,250,000 Performance Warrants (the "Warrants") to Directors and Consultants to the Corporation. The Corporation would like to correct the wording of "Performance Warrants" to "Stock Options." The terms and conditions of the Stock Options remain the same as Performance Warrants terms that were previously disclosed in the Corporation's March 7, 2025 press release.

About Bluesky Digital Assets Corp.

Bluesky Digital Assets Corp, has created a high value digital enterprise at the intersection of Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain and Web3 business solutions. Leveraging its success as an early adopter providing proprietary technology solutions, Bluesky has invested in its Roadmap. Bluesky's platform, BlueskyINTEL, is well positioned to leverage the current exponential growth of Artificial Intelligence ("AI") and Blockchain based technologies through a tightly focus built collaborative platform. This innovative web platform offering supports and better enables businesses to adopt and utilize these emerging and developing technologies.

For more information please visit Bluesky at: www.blueskydigitalassets.com or www.blueskyintel.com

Please also follow us on Linkedin at: www.linkedin.com/company/bluesky-digital-assets/

The forward-looking statements included in this document are made as of the date of this document and the Corporation disclaims any intention or obligation to update or revise any forward-looking statements, whether as a result of new information, future events or otherwise, except as expressly required by applicable securities legislation. data.title: Levi & Korsinsky Announces the Filing of a Securities Class Action on Behalf of AppLovin Corporation (APP) Shareholders
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / March 12, 2025 / If you suffered a loss on your AppLovin Corporation investment and want to learn about a potential recovery under the federal securities laws, follow the link below for more information:

https://zlk.com/pslra-1/applovin-corporation-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=135636&wire=1

or contact Joseph E. Levi, Esq. via email at jlevi@levikorsinsky.com or call (212) 363-7500 to speak to our team of experienced shareholder advocates.

THE LAWSUIT: A class action securities lawsuit was filed against AppLovin Corporation that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between May 10, 2023 and February 25, 2025.

CASE DETAILS: According to the complaint, defendants provided investors with material information concerning AppLovin's financial growth and stability. Defendants' statements included, among other things, confidence in AppLovin's launch of its AXON 2.0 digital ad platform and using "cutting-edge AI technologies" to more efficiently match advertisements to mobile games, in addition to expanding into web-based marketing and e-commerce. Moreover, defendants publicly reported impressive financial results, outlooks, and guidance to investors, all while using dishonest advertising practices.

The truth emerged on February 26, 2025, when analyst research reports emerged stating that AppLovin was reverse engineering and exploiting advertising data from Meta Platforms. The reports further alleged AppLovin was utilizing manipulative practices to artificially inflate their own ad click-through and app download rates, such as by having ads click on themselves or utilizing design gimmicks to trigger forced shadow downloads, erroneously inflating installation numbers and, in turn, its profit figures.

Following this news, the price of AppLovin's stock declined from $377.06 per share on February 25, 2025 to $331.00 per share on February 26, 2025.

WHAT'S NEXT? If you suffered a loss in AppLovin stock during the relevant time frame - even if you still hold your shares - go to https://zlk.com/pslra-1/applovin-corporation-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=135636&wire=1 to learn about your rights to seek a recovery. There is no cost or obligation to participate.

WHY LEVI & KORSINSKY: Over the past 20 years, Levi & Korsinsky LLP has established itself as a nationally-recognized securities litigation firm that has secured hundreds of millions of dollars for aggrieved shareholders and built a track record of winning high-stakes cases. The firm has extensive expertise representing investors in complex securities litigation and a team of over 70 employees to serve our clients. For seven years in a row, Levi & Korsinsky has ranked in ISS Securities Class Action Services' Top 50 Report as one of the top securities litigation firms in the United States. Attorney Advertising. Prior results do not guarantee similar outcomes.

CONTACT:

Levi & Korsinsky, LLP

Joseph E. Levi, Esq.

33 Whitehall Street, 17th Floor

New York, NY 10004

jlevi@levikorsinsky.com

data.title: Investors in Ready Capital Corporation Should Contact Levi & Korsinsky Before May 5, 2025 to Discuss Your Rights - RC
data.description:
NEW YORK CITY, NY / ACCESS Newswire / March 12, 2025 / If you suffered a loss on your Ready Capital Corporation investment and want to learn about a potential recovery under the federal securities laws, follow the link below for more information:

https://zlk.com/pslra-1/ready-capital-corporation-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=135637&wire=1

or contact Joseph E. Levi, Esq. via email at jlevi@levikorsinsky.com or call (212) 363-7500 to speak to our team of experienced shareholder advocates.

THE LAWSUIT: A class action securities lawsuit was filed against Ready Capital Corporation that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between November 7, 2024 and March 2, 2025.

CASE DETAILS: The filed complaint alleges that defendants made false statements and/or concealed that: (1) significant non-performing loans in its CRE portfolio were not likely to be collectible; (2) Ready Capital would fully reserve these problem loans in order to "stabilize" its CRE portfolio; (3) this was not accurately reflected in Ready Capital's current expected credit loss or valuation allowances; (4) as a result, the Company's financial results would be adversely affected; and (5), as a result of the foregoing, defendants' positive statements about the Company's business, operations, and prospects were materially misleading and/or lacked a reasonable basis.

WHAT'S NEXT? If you suffered a loss in Ready Capital stock during the relevant time frame - even if you still hold your shares - go to https://zlk.com/pslra-1/ready-capital-corporation-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=135637&wire=1 to learn about your rights to seek a recovery. Algoma Steel Says Tariffs Will Hurt Finances, Operations — Market Talk
data.description: Algoma Steel Group says that tariffs on steel and aluminum will have a material and adverse effect on its finances and operations. That is because tariffs have driven steel prices below production costs for much of the industry, as well as heightened general macroeconomic uncertainty. "We remain confident that the Canadian federal and provincial governments will respond swiftly and appropriately to support the industry," CEO Michael Garcia says. "Given the deeply integrated North American supply chain, we believe rational dialogue will prevail between these close allies, restoring normal steel trade." The Canadian company is currently assessing the tariffs, adding that it is too early to estimate the full extent of their effects at this time. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: New York State's Housing Finance Agency Plans $113.7 Million Bond Sale
data.description:
By Josh Beckerman

The New York State Housing Finance Agency plans to sell $113.7 million of affordable housing revenue bonds to finance mortgage loans for multi-family rental housing.

The sale is expected to include $23.5 million of 2025 Series A-1 bonds, with the majority of that amount offered as term bonds maturing from 2040 through 2068, according to a preliminary official statement posted on MuniOS. Serial bonds maturing from 2028 through 2037 will also be sold as part of the offering.

The agency will also offer $90.2 million of 2025 Series A-2 bonds in the form of two bonds maturing in 2064. Those securities have mandatory tender dates of May 2029 and May 2030.

Pricing is scheduled for March 20, and the securities are expected to be delivered March 27. The bonds are special revenue obligations and will be secured by revenue, funds and accounts under a general resolution and by program assets.

Proceeds from the sale will be used to finance two mortgage loans that finance the creation, rehabilitation or preservation of 343 units of affordable housing. One project is a new 18-story building at the Edgemere Commons, a mixed-use project on the Rockaway peninsula in Queens. The other is for the preservation and rehabilitation of Saratoga Heights, located in Binghamton.

The agency is a public benefit corporation that finances low- and moderate-income rental housing.

Moody's rated the bonds Aa2.

BofA Securities is lead underwriter.

Write to Josh Beckerman at josh.beckerman@wsj.com

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1853 ET - Algoma Steel Group says that tariffs on steel and aluminum will have a material and adverse effect on its finances and operations. That is because tariffs have driven steel prices below production costs for much of the industry, as well as heightened general macroeconomic uncertainty. "We remain confident that the Canadian federal and provincial governments will respond swiftly and appropriately to support the industry," CEO Michael Garcia says. "Given the deeply integrated North American supply chain, we believe rational dialogue will prevail between these close allies, restoring normal steel trade." The Canadian company is currently assessing the tariffs, adding that it is too early to estimate the full extent of their effects at this time. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1417 ET - Lawson Whiting, chief executive of Jack Daniel's maker Brown-Forman, says that the European Union's retaliatory tariffs on imports of American whiskey, among other goods, aren't a surprise. "There's been a lot of signals going on for a long time, particularly coming from the EU," he says at the UBS Global Consumer and Retail Conference. "It's a tough spot," Whiting says, adding that it is good news the tariffs don't go into effect until April 1. "We've got a few weeks to be able to try to get these parties together and resolve the differences on this particular tariff conversation," he says. "It does give me some optimism that both the administration and the other counterparties over the last few weeks have been trying to resolve things relatively quickly." Brown-Forman off more than 5%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1412 ET - The U.S. imposed 25% tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports earlier today, but President Trump had threatened a 50% tariff on Canadian steel and aluminum Tuesday, before relenting. That additional tariff would likely change trade flows, but not in a way that would benefit U.S. manufacturing, Alcoa CFO Molly Beerman says at the JPMorgan Industrials Conference. Under proposed higher tariffs, Alcoa would most likely send its aluminum produced in Canada to Europe, she says. "That supply may very well make its way over to the U.S.," Beerman says, adding that to have ships crossing the Atlantic with the same product just to get the right tariff treatment would be very disruptive. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1343 ET - Alcoa "would not make a decision on building a new smelter based on tariffs," CFO Molly Beerman says at the JPMorgan Industrials Conference. "These are decisions that you're looking out 20 to 40 years." The company could restart some of its idle U.S. capacity, she says, though she adds that those smelters have not been run for a very long time and would be very expensive to restart. "We are looking at the numbers but we'll want to see the cost to restart, as well as how the tariffs stick before we make a decision on whether we'll bring that back online," Beerman says. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1318 ET - February's CPI brings "subtle notes of tariff pass-through," BNP Paribas' Andy Schneider and Britney Jackson write, looking into categories where imports are relevant. Core goods excluding used vehicles rose 0.12% in the month, compared to a flat prior three-month average and its strongest monthly pace since September. "Apparel (particularly jewelry and watches), personal care products and miscellaneous personal goods prices were firm," they say, adding that there was "minor movement in other goods categories that we flagged as sensitive to Chinese imports such as household furnishings, consumer electronics and toys." Schneider and Jackson expect greater upward movement in these categories this month. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1303 ET - Alcoa has two smelters in the U.S. that produce about 290,000 metric tons of aluminum annually, both of which stand to benefit from tariffs enacted on imports of steel and aluminum, CFO Molly Beerman says at the JPMorgan Industrials Conference. "However, our Canadian operations produce three times that amount," she says. A 25% tariff on the aluminum that Alcoa produces in Canada would be a net negative for the company, resulting in the loss of tens of millions of dollars, Beerman says. The loss isn't likely to hurt the company's first quarter, but could affect future earnings, she adds. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1258 ET - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper up 1% at $9,771.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.1% at $2,701.50 a ton. Markets have been rocked by President Trump's tariffs, Trade Nation's David Morrison says in a note. After Canadian, Mexican and Chinese levies, Wednesday saw the imposition of additional 25% tariffs on U.S. imports of aluminum and steel. The price of U.S. aluminum over LME prices has surged in response as traders scramble to import the metal. There have been tariff postponements, exceptions and retaliation and investors are struggling to keep up, Morrison writes. But the overall sense is that tariffs are no longer just a threat that precedes negotiations, Morrison says. Under the current administration, they are a weapon--however blunt--that Trump is prepared to employ, he says. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1254 ET - Alcoa is working closely with President Trump's administration, as well as trade groups in the U.S. and Canada, to decipher the effect that tariffs on steel and aluminum will have on the industry, CFO Molly Beerman says at the J.P. Morgan Industrials Conference. "While we're very supportive of (the administration's) efforts to improve the industry, as well as strengthen U.S. manufacturing jobs, we do see that there could be some harm from the tariffs," she says, adding that Alcoa is particularly focused on gaining a Canadian exemption. The company is also having discussions about energy policy, because "affordable and access to energy, particularly renewable, is critical in any decision for additional aluminum investment in the long term," Beerman says. Shares rise 3.3%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1251 ET - Tariffs on steel and aluminum are already creating significant uncertainty in the market, Alcoa CFO Molly Beerman says at the JPMorgan Industrials Conference. "Some customers are rushing to secure supply ahead of the tariffs, while others are playing wait and see, to see how the final tariff structure will exist," she says. "As a result this quarter, we do have more uncertainty in both our revenue and our working capital that is more than normal." Overall, though, Alcoa is expecting what Beerman calls a strong first quarter, boosted by higher costs for the metal. Shares rise 3%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1216 ET - Bank of Canada Gov. Tiff Macklem says officials are determined to not let inflation accelerate beyond the 2% target during this time of trade tension. Most of the central bank's communications, alongside its decision to make a quarter-point rate cut, is focused on the risk of higher prices that tariffs bring. He says some prices will go up due to tariffs, in contrast to arguments from Trump administration officials. "What we don't want to see," Macklem says at a press conference, "is that first round of price increases have knock-on effects, causing other prices to go up. That becomes generalized and ongoing inflation. That's what we can't let happen here." (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1148 ET - President Trump's new tariffs on aluminum and steel will take a toll on the entire auto industry, with some of the biggest price surges expected for larger-model vehicles like pickup trucks and SUVs, Kevin Roberts of CarGurus says in a note. The scale of the price hikes will depend on each automaker's sourcing and production chain, he says, but vehicles that use more materials will see the biggest markups, Roberts says. The levies will have a more immediate impact on the prices of used cars if the cost of auto parts goes up, he says. The used market is more supply-restrained, especially given high demand for vehicles that are three years old or younger, so anyone considering those models should purchase sooner rather than later, Roberts says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1103 ET - Live cattle futures on the CME are up 1.1%, pushing the most-active futures contract over the $2.01 a pound mark. For cattle, supportive fundamentals are striking a balance with an increasingly stormier macroeconomic view. "Traders being pushed from two directions," says ADM Investor Services in a note. "One side has boxed beef prices rapidly rising but at the same time traders are alarmed there is talk about a recession and financial markets falling." New retaliatory tariffs from the EU include beef and pork products. Lean hogs are up 0.1%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

GreenX Metals' Fiscal H1 Loss Widens
data.description:
GreenX Metals reported Wednesday that its loss per diluted share widened to AU$0.0075 in the fiscal first half from AU$0.0073 a year earlier.

Interest income for the six months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was AU$141,391, down from AU$252,221 in the same period a year earlier.

data.title: UNISYNC Announces Voting Results of AGM
data.description:
TORONTO, March 12, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Unisync Corp. (“Unisync") (TSX:"UNI") (OTC:“USYNF”) announces the voting results of the Annual General Meeting of its shareholders held today in Vancouver, British Columbia.

The following five existing directors were reelected as directors of the Company for the ensuing year by the following votes out of a total of 7,153,982 shares voted, representing 37.63% of total shares outstanding:

NomineeVotes ForPercentVotes WithheldPercent
Darryl R. Eddy7,049,08299.53%33,30000.47%
Douglas F. Good6,255,49788.32%826,88511.68%
Tim Gu6,288,79788.79%793,58511.21%
Joel R. McLean7,082,382100.00%000.00%
Michael Rossi7,082,382100.00%000.00%

In addition, MNP LLP was re-appointed as the auditor of the Company for the ensuing year and the Company’s unallocated stock options issuable pursuant to the Corporation’s stock option plan was approved for a further period of three years.

Former director Bruce Aunger who has been a valuable member of the board since November of 2005, provided notice of his resignation prior to the meeting. We want to thank Bruce for his valuable contribution to the board over close to 20 years and wish him well in his future endeavours.

On Behalf of the Board of Directors

Douglas F. Good

CEO

Investor relations contact:

Douglas F Good at 778-370-1725 Email: dgood@unisyncgroup.com
